coredata.dc:title: An “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor based on Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs for hexavalent chromium detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Xiao, Zhenfang
Huang, Qiumei
Huang, Wenyi
Zhang, Guoqiang
Li, Dongdong
Zhang, Zhuwei
Cheng, Hao
Feng, Jun
Li, Lijun
coredata.dc:description: The problems of poor water solubility, poor stability, and poor selectivity encountered in the determination of hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) in water using ZnO QDs need to be addressed. In this study, we successfully prepared Sm-doped, –NH2-modified Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs via the sol–gel method. Sm doping was used to enhance the fluorescence intensity of ZnO QDs, while 3-aminopropyltrietoxysilane (APTEs) capping improved their water solubility and fluorescence stability. The fluorescence of Sm:ZnO–NH2 quantum dots was quenched after the addition of Cr(VI) due to the internal filtration effect(IFE), and was restored after the addition of ascorbic acid due to the redox reaction between ascorbic acid (AA) and Cr(VI). Leveraging the fluorescence response patterns of the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs system when exposed to Cr(VI) and AA, we developed an ’’on-off-on’’ fluorescent sensor that can specifically detect Cr(VI) and AA without interference from Cu2+ ions. The “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor exhibited a linear response to Cr(VI) concentrations ranging from 0.05 to 1.5 μg/mL, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 6.15 ng/mL. It exhibited excellent selectivity and repeatability. Furthermore, the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs fluorescent sensor was effectively utilized for detecting Cr(VI) in tap water, offering a new method for heavy metal detection via an “on-off-on” fluorescence switching mechanism.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs
“On-off-on” fluorescent probe
Cr(VI)
Internal filtering effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of Cr(VI) and AA
Effect of reaction time and Sm doping on the fluorescence properties of Sm:ZnO QDs
Fluorescence burst mechanism of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs
Specificity of fluorescence recovery of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs
Effect of pH, response time and NaCl concentration on the fluorescence properties of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs
Reproducibility, selectivity and interference of fluorescent probes for Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs
Detection of Cr(VI) and AA in actual samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of Sm:ZnO QDs with APTEs: before capping (a) at 100 nm scale, (b) at 50 nm scale; after capping (c) at 100 nm scale, (d) at 50 nm scale, (e) at 20 nm scale; (f) HRTEM image of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs (The inset is the electron diffraction map of the selected region); (g, h, i, j, k) EDS elemental mapping images of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs; Particle size distribution of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs prepared with different APTEs concentrations: (l) 0 mol/L, (m) 0.05 mol/L; (n) X-ray diffractograms of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs with different Sm doping levels (0–5 %); (o) FT-IR spectrum of Sm:ZnO QDs before and after coating with APTEs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cr(VI), Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs, Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cr(VI); (b) Fluorescence spectra of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs and Abs of Cr(VI); (c) Zeta potential values of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs, Cr(VI), AA, Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cr(VI) and Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cr(VI) + AA; (d) Fluorescence lifetime decay curve of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs and Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cr(VI).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra for fluorescence recovery sensing (A: Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs, B: Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cr(VI), C: Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + AA, D: Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cr(VI) + AA Cr(VI): 1.5 μg/mL, AA: 30 ng/mL, E: AA); Fluorescence emission spectra of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cr(VI) system with and without AA (b) and Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cu2+ system with and without AA (c).

(a) Spectra of fluorescence for Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs with varying Cr(VI) concentrations; (b) Linear relationship between different concentrations of Cr(VI) and (F0-F)/F0 (Cr(VI) 0–1.5 μg/mL, F0 denotes the fluorescence intensity of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs without the addition of Cr(VI), and F denotes the fluorescence intensity of the system with different concentrations of Cr(VI)); (c) Fluorescence spectra of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cr(VI) system in the presence of different concentrations of AA; (d) Linear relationship between different concentrations of AA and (F’−F’0)/F’0 (F’ and F’0 denote the fluorescence intensities of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cr(VI) system with and without AA, respectively).
Comparison of different fluorescence methods for the detection of Cr(VI) and AA.
Determination of Cr(VI) (n = 3) in actual water samples by standard addition method.
Determination of AA (n = 3) in actual water samples by standard addition method.

head.ce:title: An “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor based on Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs for hexavalent chromium detection

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The problems of poor water solubility, poor stability, and poor selectivity encountered in the determination of hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) in water using ZnO QDs need to be addressed. In this study, we successfully prepared Sm-doped, –NH2-modified Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs via the sol–gel method. Sm doping was used to enhance the fluorescence intensity of ZnO QDs, while 3-aminopropyltrietoxysilane (APTEs) capping improved their water solubility and fluorescence stability. The fluorescence of Sm:ZnO–NH2 quantum dots was quenched after the addition of Cr(VI) due to the internal filtration effect(IFE), and was restored after the addition of ascorbic acid due to the redox reaction between ascorbic acid (AA) and Cr(VI). Leveraging the fluorescence response patterns of the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs system when exposed to Cr(VI) and AA, we developed an ’’on-off-on’’ fluorescent sensor that can specifically detect Cr(VI) and AA without interference from Cu2+ ions. The “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor exhibited a linear response to Cr(VI) concentrations ranging from 0.05 to 1.5 μg/mL, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 6.15 ng/mL. It exhibited excellent selectivity and repeatability. Furthermore, the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs fluorescent sensor was effectively utilized for detecting Cr(VI) in tap water, offering a new method for heavy metal detection via an “on-off-on” fluorescence switching mechanism.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of industry, a significant quantity of toxic heavy metal ions have been discharged into the natural environment, resulting in severe contamination of water bodies and soils, which poses a threat to human life and health. In its trivalent (Cr(III)) and hexavalent (Cr(VI)) forms, chromium (Cr) is a toxic metal frequently encountered in the environment, where Cr(VI) exhibits toxicity levels 500 to 1,000 times greater than those of Cr(III) [1,2]. Chromium (VI) has significant disadvantages owing to its high toxicity, carcinogenicity, persistent environmental hazards, and difficulty in being effectively detected in aquatic environments [3]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a method to accurately detect Cr(VI) in the environment.

To date, researchers have developed various methods for detecting Cr(VI), including electrochemical [4,5], chromatographic [6,7], colorimetric [8,9], and fluorescent techniques [10,11]. Among the various methodologies, the fluorescent probe technique is particularly esteemed for its notable benefits, including cost-effectiveness, high sensitivity, and rapid detection capabilities [12–14]. A range of fluorescent sensors, encompassing polymeric organics [15], metallic nanoclusters [3], metal–organic frameworks constructs (MOFs) [16], covalent organic frameworks structures (COFs) [17], and quantum dots (QDs) [18], have been employed for the identification of Cr(VI). Liu et al. [3] developed a fluorescence technique using gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) to quantitatively assess Cr(VI), based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between AuNCs and Cr(VI). Wang et al. [19] created a quantitative fluorescent sensor using a carbon dot-encapsulated europium-based metal–organic framework to measure hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)), based on the internal filtration effect (IFE) between the carbon dots and Cr(VI). However, these fluorescent probes are susceptible to toxicity [20], exhibit low quantum yields [21], possess complex structures [22], and lack sufficient stability [23], thereby limiting their application for the rapid detection of Cr(VI).

ZnO QDs, which possess unique physicochemical properties, exhibit significant advantages in the detection of heavy metal ions in the environment owing to their low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and excellent optical properties [24]. Research has demonstrated that ZnO QDs exhibit a high sensitivity for Cr(VI) detection [25]. However, unmodified ZnO QDs are challenging to stably store unmodified ZnO QDs in aqueous solutions, and their fluorescence performance is susceptible to environmental factors as well as interference from other ions [26]. Therefore, enhancing the fluorescence stability of ZnO QDs in aqueous solutions and improving the selectivity and sensitivity of the fluorescence analysis method for ZnO QDs are particularly important.

Currently, the fluorescence method for detecting Cr(VI) typically employs an ''on-off'' mode of fluorescence response, however, this mode is susceptible to interference from the surrounding environment, thereby reducing the accuracy and sensitivity of the detection method [27]. Recently, inspired by a fluorescence burst–recovery response—specifically, the concept of restoring luminescence in a fluorescent probe upon the addition of a new substance [28], we have engineered a fluorescent sensor with an ''on-off-on'' mechanism to boost the detection sensitivity and specificity for Cr(VI).

In this study, we successfully synthesized Sm-doped ZnO–NH2 QDs by incorporating the rare-earth element Sm to significantly augment the fluorescent characteristics of ZnO QDs, and furthermore, we uniformly coated them with 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTEs) to enhance the stability of their fluorescence in aqueous environments. Leveraging the burst–recovery fluorescence characteristics of the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs in the presence of Cr(VI) and ascorbic acid (AA), we crafted an ''on-off-on'' fluorescent sensor tailored for the selective detection of Cr(VI) and AA. This approach presented a novel paradigm for detecting heavy metal ions by employing the ''on-off-on'' fluorescence switching mechanism.
The supporting information section offers comprehensive details regarding the materials and reagents used.

A solution of NaOH in ethanol was prepared by dissolving 0.01 mol of sodium hydroxide (NaOH) in anhydrous ethanol (100 mL) at 50 °C. 0.01 mol of Zn(Ac)2·2H2O and a specific quantity of Sm(NO3)3·6H2O were fully dissolved in 100 mL of anhydrous ethanol while maintaining the solution temperature at 50 °C. Subsequently, an adequate amount of PEG 200 was added and stirred for 10 min. Next, the previously prepared NaOH ethanol solution was added, and stirring was continued at 50 °C to yield a solution containing Sm:ZnO QDs.

To the Sm:ZnO QDs solution obtained previously, 10 mL of a 0.05 M APTEs solution in ethanol and 0.4 mL of ultrapure water were added. The mixture was agitated at ambient temperature for 2 h. After dialysis of the product for 48 h to remove impurities, the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs were obtained(Scheme 1 ). The QDs were dispersed in ultrapure water and stored at 4 °C for future use.
The supporting material provides a detailed description of the instrumentation used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Cr(VI) and AA
ce:section.ce:para: The supporting material presents the specifics of this section.

The TEM was utilized to characterize the morphology and crystalline structures of both Sm:ZnO QDs and Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs. The unencapsulated Sm:ZnO QDs exhibited significant agglomeration, as shown in Fig. 1 (a, b). In contrast, Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs encapsulated with APTEs exhibited improved dispersion and more uniform particle sizes, as shown in Fig. 1(c-e), the average diameter of these QDs was approximately 5 nm. A comparison of (a) and (c) or (b) and (d) in Fig. 1 indicates that the surface capping of the QDs with APTEs reduced their agglomeration and enhanced their dispersion performance in aqueous solutions. HRTEM was used to characterize the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs and further analyze their crystalline nature. The electron diffraction pattern for a specific area was displayed in the inset of Fig. 1(f). Analysis of the HRTEM images showed that the crystallite spacings were 0.18 nm and 0.26 nm, which matched the (102) and (100) crystallites of fibrous zincate-type ZnO [29,30] The Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs were analyzed elementally, as shown in Fig. 1(g-k). The analysis revealed that they mainly contained Zn, Sm, Si, N, and O. These results confirmed the successful preparation of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs.

DLS was employed to analyze the particle size distribution of the Sm:ZnO QDs both before and after capping with APTEs. Fig. 1(l) and 1(m) depict the average diameter of uncapped Sm:ZnO QDs, which was estimated to be around 6.61 nm, whereas that of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs after capping with APTEs was approximately 5.09 nm. A comparison of Fig. 1(l) and (m) reveals that the particle size of the uncapped QDs was larger than that of the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs owing to the propensity of the uncapped QDs to agglomerate. Furthermore, Sm:ZnO QDs modified with –NH2 were well dispersed in water. The aforementioned results agreed with the TEM analysis.

To investigate the impact of Sm doping on the QDs, experimentally prepared Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs were characterized using XRD. Fig. 1(n) shows the XRD patterns of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs with Sm doping concentrations ranging from 0 % to 5 %. As depicted in Fig. 1(n), the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs, with Sm doping concentrations ranging from 0 % to 5 %, exhibited distinct diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 31.777°, 36.261°, 47.543°, 56.611°, 62.860°, and 67.962°. Notably, there was no significant alteration in the intensity or shape of the XRD peaks as the Sm doping concentration increased. Compared with the standard card (COD 9011662) for hexagonal wurtzite-type ZnO, the crystal planes of ZnO corresponding to the aforementioned diffraction peaks were the (100), (101), (102), (110), (103), and (112). The presence of these crystal planes indicated that the ZnO was hexagonal fibrillar zincite-type [31]. No characteristic diffraction peaks corresponding to Sm oxide or other phases were observed in the XRD patterns of the samples. This indicated that the prepared samples exhibited high purity, with no detectable impurities other than ZnO. This was likely due to the successful doping of Sm3+ ions, which replaced Zn2+ ions within the ZnO lattice without causing any significant alterations in the ZnO lattice structure [31–33]. The structural units of Sm: ZnO QDs and Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs were analyzed using FT-IR. Fig. 1(o) exhibits an absorption band at 455 cm−1, which was associated with the stretching vibration of the Zn–O bond in the ZnO lattice [34]. Compared to the Sm:ZnO QDs, the absorption peak observed at 2925 cm−1 in the spectrum of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs corresponded to the stretching vibration of the N–H bond in the APTEs. Meanwhile, the broad absorption peak observed at 3340 cm−1 was attributed to the overlap between the O–H stretching vibration peak located on the surface of the QDs and the stretching vibration of the N–H bonds [35]. The 1415 cm−1 absorption band was associated with the symmetric stretching mode of the CO bond. Concurrently, the absorption band at 1068 cm−1 was linked to the stretching vibration of the Si–O bond, which was derived from the APTEs [36]. Analysis of the FT-IR spectra revealed that the surface of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs functionalized with APTEs was successfully modified to incorporate –NH2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of reaction time and Sm doping on the fluorescence properties of Sm:ZnO QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation conditions for the QDs, including the reaction time and doping amounts of Sm, were optimized experimentally. Initially, experiments were conducted to optimize the reaction time for the synthesis of QDs. As shown in Fig. S1(a), upon excitation at 355 nm, the fluorescence intensity of the ZnO QDs exhibited an initial rise followed by a decline as the reaction time increased. The maximum fluorescence intensity was observed at the reaction time of 15 min, which was attributed to the formation time and surface defects of the ZnO QDs. Prior to 15 min of reaction, the ZnO QDs did not react completely, and the fluorescence intensity increased progressively with increasing reaction time, reaching a maximum value at 15 min. Prolonging the reaction time resulted in a notably reduced fluorescence intensity. This was attributed to the agglomeration of ZnO QDs and subsequent reduction in surface defects, which in turn led to a decrease in fluorescence [37]. Therefore, the reaction time of the ZnO QDs was determined to be 15 min and proceeded to the next studies.

Sm doping exerted an influence on the fluorescence properties of QDs. Fig. S1(b) illustrates that the fluorescence intensity of the QDs rose markedly at first and subsequently declined as the concentration of Sm dopant was elevated. This suggested that incorporating a moderate amount of Sm into QDs can enhance their fluorescence intensity, which was attributed to the alteration of the V-O concentration on the QDs surface as a result of Sm doping [38]. Sm doping increased the defect concentration of ZnO QDs. Sm3+ ions occupied the positions of Zn2+ ions, and the polarizability of Sm3+ affected the growth of ZnO QDs crystals, which in turn led to a change in the size of ZnO QDs [39]. With Sm3+ doping, the size of ZnO QDs decreased, and the specific surface area increased accordingly. This change promoted the formation of more vacancies and defects, thereby significantly enhancing the fluorescence intensity of ZnO QDs [40].Therefore, Sm doping of 3 % was selected for the next study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence burst mechanism of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The objective of this research was to explore the reason behind the fluorescence enhancement observed between Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs and Cr(VI). We conducted a series of experiments to measure the UV absorption spectra of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs with and without Cr(VI) and Cr(VI), excitation spectra (Ex) of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs, emission spectra (Em) of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs, and fluorescence lifetimes of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs in existence and non-existence of Cr(VI). The results were presented in Fig. 2 . Common bursting mechanisms include static bursting (SB), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and the internal filtration effect (IFE) [41–44].

Fig. 2(a) illustrates that the UV absorption profile of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs with Cr(VI) was due to the overlap of the UV absorption spectra from both Cr(VI) and the QDs themselves. Notably, there was a significant overlap in the UV absorption region near 355 nm between the spectra of Cr(VI) and Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs. This result indicated that no new substances were formed in the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cr(VI) system [45], ruling out SB. It was also indicated that the fluorescence burst observed in Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs upon exposure to Cr(VI) may be attributed to the IFE [41–43]. This typically happens when the absorption spectrum of the bursting agent intersects with either the excitation or emission spectrum of the QDs [46]. Fig. 2(b) displays the excitation peak at 355 nm and the emission peak at 569 nm for Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs. Notably, the maximum absorption peak of Cr(VI) in the UV region overlapped with the fluorescence excitation peak of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs. Thus, the mechanism responsible for the fluorescence bursting observed in Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs induced by Cr(VI) may be attributed to IFE [45,47]. In addition, the surface charge of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs was probed. As shown in Fig. 2(c), Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs exhibited a surface potential of + 17.1 mV in aqueous solution, indicating a positive charge. Meanwhile, the potentials of Cr(VI) and AA were −13.8 mV and −12.8 mV, respectively. When Cr(VI) was added to the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs solution, the surface potential of the composite decreased to + 11.5 mV. This decrease was attributed to the negative charge of Cr(VI) in aqueous solution, primarily in the form of HCrO4 − and CrO4 2−. These negatively charged species can electrostatically adsorb onto the positively charged Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs, thereby facilitating the IFE [48]. This reduction in surface charge led to a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the composite. As seen in Fig. 2(c), the surface charge of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cr(VI) + AA increased to +13.7 mV. This increase was due to AA acting as a reducing agent, converting Cr(VI) to Cr(III). This conversion reduced the overall surface charge effect of the composite, thereby restoring its fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence lifetime served as an effective parameter for distinguishing the presence of IFE, and this study further investigated the fluorescence lifetime of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs in the presence and absence of Cr(VI). The results shown in Fig. 2(d) reveal no notable alteration in the fluorescence lifetime, reinforcing the conclusion that Cr(VI)'s presence did not modify the radiation energy of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs. This eliminated the possibility of FRET and PET. In summary, the observed fluorescence bursting in Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs upon exposure to Cr(VI) was attributed to electrostatic adsorption and IFE.

The mechanism underlying the fluorescence burst–recovery phenomenon observed in Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs was experimentally investigated. Fig. 3 (a) indicates that the incorporation of Cr(VI) into the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs solution resulted in a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity. Subsequently, fluorescence of the system was restored by the addition of AA. As AA did not exhibit fluorescence, this implied that the observed fluorescence recovery was not attributed to AA emission. Furthermore, the peak positions of the fluorescence spectra remained unchanged before the occurrence of the fluorescence burst as well as after the restoration of fluorescence, suggesting that the introduction of AA did not lead to the generation of novel fluorescent species within the system. Hence, the fluorescence recovery observed can be ascribed to the redox interaction between Cr(VI) and AA upon introduction into the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cr(VI) system. Consequently, Cr(VI) was reduced to Cr(III), leading to the elimination of the IFE between Cr(VI) and Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs, thereby restoring the fluorescence of the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity of fluorescence recovery of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, we found that both Cr(VI) and Cu2+ exerted fluorescence bursting effects on Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs. Therefore, we added AA to the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cr(VI) and Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cu2+ systems to study fluorescence recovery. As shown in Fig. 3(b), after the addition of AA, a notable fluorescence recovery effect was observed in the system consisting of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs and Cr(VI). In contrast, the fluorescence of the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cu2+ system remained almost unchanged with and without AA(Fig. 3(c)). These results indicated the specificity of the fluorescence recovery effect of AA on the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cr(VI) system. Therefore, based on the specificity of the fluorescence burst–recovery of the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cr(VI) + AA system, we can develop an ''on-off-on'' fluorescent probe for the specific recognition of Cr(VI) and AA and establish a fluorescent analytical detection method with good selectivity and high sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH, response time and NaCl concentration on the fluorescence properties of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: To enhance the detection performance of the sensor, the effect of pH variations on the fluorescence intensity of the QDs was investigated. Fig. S2(a) illustrates that the pH was adjusted using a Tri-HCl buffer solution in the experiment to study how it affected the fluorescence stabilization of QDs within the pH range of 6 to 8. At varying pH levels, the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs fluorescence sensor displayed distinct fluorescence emission intensities. The fluorescence intensity reached its peak at a pH of 7.0, whereas the fluorescence intensity of the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cr(VI) system remained basically unchanged and approached a state of complete burst across various pH values. On this basis, the fluorescence began to recover when AA was added to the system of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cr(VI), and it was found that fluorescence began to recover. The observed phenomenon was due to the combination of Cr(VI)'s potent oxidizing ability and AA's reducing capacity, leading to the conversion of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) upon the addition of AA. At a pH of 7.0, the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cr(VI) + AA system exhibited the highest fluorescence recovery intensity. The fluorescence intensity of the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cr(VI) + AA system decreased when the pH was less than or greater than 7.0. A possible reason for this was that the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs system was unstable under acidic or alkaline conditions, and the environment tended to burst fluorescence [24]. Furthermore, Fig. S2(b) depicts the examination of the impact of response time on the QDs' performance in fluorescence detection. Within a response time of 2 min, Cr(VI) fully quenched the fluorescence of the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs, whereas AA fully restored the fluorescence of the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cr(VI) system within the same timeframe and sustained a stable fluorescence intensity thereafter. The effect of the NaCl concentration on the fluorescence detection performance of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs was also explored. As depicted in Fig. S2(c), across varying NaCl concentrations, the fluorescence emission intensity of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs remained virtually unchanged, suggesting that NaCl concentration had no impact on the intensity of their fluorescence. This further demonstrated the excellent anti-interference capability of Sm: ZnO–NH2 QDs towards salt solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility, selectivity and interference of fluorescent probes for Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: To ensure the reliability of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs as fluorescent probes for quantitative assay results, the reproducibility of the test method was examined. Fig. S3(a) shows the reproducibility of the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs fluorescent sensor prepared from the same batch used for the parallel detection of Cr(VI) eight times, three times in parallel for each sample, with relative standard deviation (RSD) values less than 1 %. The RSD was calculated by averaging the test results for the eight samples and was 0.47 %. Fig. S3(b) shows the reproducible detection results of the fluorescence recovery of the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cr(VI) sensors prepared from the same batch after the addition of AA. The RSD was calculated by averaging the test results of the eight samples, and the RSD was 4.01 %. The above results indicated that the application of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs as fluorescent sensors for Cr(VI) and AA detection had excellent reproducibility.

In Fig. S4, the selectivity of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs for Cr(VI) detection was assessed using ΔF = F0-F1, where F0 represents the fluorescence intensity of the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs probe, and F1 denotes the fluorescence intensity after the addition of Mn+ to the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs. According to Fig. S4(a), none of the interfering ions, including Ag+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, K+, Na+, Cd2+, and Al3+, exhibited a notable impact on the fluorescence intensity of the Sm: ZnO–NH2 QDs, with the exception of Cu2+. Hence, we introduced AA into the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Mn+ system in the presence of interfering ions to investigate its fluorescence recovery response. The findings were depicted in Fig. S4(b), where ΔF’=F2-F1, where F2 is the fluorescence intensity of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Mn+ + AA. As shown in Fig. S4(b), among all the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Mn+ + AA systems, only the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cr(VI) + AA system showed significant fluorescence enhancement, and the fluorescence intensities of the other Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Mn+ + AA systems were basically unchanged, indicating that AA produced obvious fluorescence recovery only for the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cr(VI) system. In contrast, the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Mn+ system did not produce fluorescence recovery for Ag+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, K+, Na+, Cd2+, Al3+, and Cu2+. Consequently, the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cr(VI) + AA system enabled the exclusion of Cu2+ interference during Cr(VI) detection, allowing for specific recognition of Cr(VI).

To investigate the selectivity of AA for fluorescence recovery of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cr(VI), possible interfering substances (folic acid, uric acid, glutathione, L-tyrosine, alanine, glutamic acid, glucose, L-histidine, L-arginine, and L-cysteine) were added to the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cr(VI) system. Fig. S5 displays the outcomes, demonstrating that possible interfering agents exerted a negligible effect on the fluorescence restoration response of the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cr(VI) assembly. Consequently, this system demonstrated excellent selectivity for AA detection.

We investigated the variation in fluorescence intensity of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs in response to a range of Cr(VI) concentrations. Fig. 4 (a) illustrates that with escalating Cr(VI) concentrations, there was a corresponding decline in the fluorescence intensity of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs, while the shape and position of the fluorescence emission peaks remained unchanged. As shown in Fig. 4(b), a linear regression analysis was performed on the Cr(VI) concentration, utilizing the ratio (F0–F)/F0 as the basis for the analysis. Within the concentration span of 0.05 to 1.5 μg/mL, Cr(VI) demonstrated a strong linear correlation with (F0-F)/F0, following the linear regression equation y = 0.59775x + 0.01869 and achieving a coefficient of determination R2 of 0.99962. With a signal-to-noise ratio of 3S/N, the limit of detection (LOD) for Cr(VI) was ascertained as 6.15 ng/mL, and the RSD was below 0.12 %. This finding demonstrated that the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs fluorescence sensor exhibited excellent reproducibility when used for detecting Cr(VI) solutions. Additionally, this study conducted a comparative analysis of our method with alternative approaches for AA detection. As shown in Table 1 , the LOD and linear range of this work were better than those of the other detection methods, indicating that the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs had a low LOD and high sensitivity for Cr (VI) detection.

We also examined the fluorescence recovery behavior of the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cr(VI) system with AA. Fig. 4(c) displays that as the concentration of AA rose, the fluorescence of the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cr(VI) system progressively regained its intensity. Fig. 4(d) shows the working curve of AA, and a linear regression analysis was performed by creating a standard curve for the concentration of AA with (F-F0)/F0 as the vertical coordinate, which exhibited a strong linear relationship within the concentration range from 1 to 30 ng/mL. The linear equation was y = 0.29793 x + 0.00178, R2 = 0.99937, and the LOD of AA was 0.11 ng/mL at 3S/N with an RSD less than 1.05 %. Furthermore, comparing this method with other methods for detecting AA, as listed in Table 1, the method proposed in this study for detecting AA had a lower LOD, indicating higher sensitivity and resolution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr(VI) and AA in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate the feasibility and accuracy of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs in detecting Cr(VI) and the combined system of Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs with Cr(VI) in detecting AA, real samples such as tap water and orange juice were selected for analysis. The spiked recoveries were examined using the standard addition method (Tables 2 and 3
). Table 2 shows that the recoveries of Cr(VI) in tap water ranged from 98.92 % to 101.03 %, with an RSD of 0.14 % to 0.2 %. Similarly, Table 3 indicates that the recoveries of AA in orange juice fell within the range of 98.89 % to 100.41 %, accompanied by an RSD of 0.33 % to 1.94 %. The results confirmed the viability of using the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs and Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cr(VI) fluorescent sensors for detecting Cr(VI) in tap water and AA in orange juice, respectively.

In this study, we successfully prepared Sm-doped, –NH2-modified Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs as fluorescent probes. We enhanced the fluorescence intensity of ZnO QDs through doping with the rare-earth element Sm and improved the fluorescence stability of ZnO QDs in aqueous solutions by utilizing APTEs encapsulation. Based on the fluorescence burst–recovery characteristics of the Sm:ZnO–NH2 QDs + Cr(VI) + AA system, we developed an ''on-off-on'' fluorescence analysis method using an ''on-off-on'' fluorescence strategy. The method was capable of rapidly detecting Cr(VI), and its fluorescence intensity could be rapidly restored by the addition of AA. Furthermore, this method effectively excluded the interference of Cu2+. The analytical method was characterized by a low LOD (6.15 ng/mL) and a wide linear range (0.05–1.5 μg/mL), and exhibited high selectivity and reproducibility. Additionally, the application of this fluorescent sensor for detecting Cr(VI) in real water samples demonstrated high recovery rates and accuracy. The method was simple, rapid, sensitive, and reproducible, providing a new approach for the development of heavy metal ion detection using the ''on-off-on'' fluorescence strategy. It also shows significant potential for applications in environmental monitoring and biomedical diagnostics.

APTEs, 3-aminopropyltrietoxysilane; Cr(VI), Hexavalent Chromium; AA, ascorbic acid; TEM, transmission electron spectroscopy; XRD, X-ray diffraction; FT-IR, Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer; DLS, Dynamic Light Scattering instrument.

Zhenfang Xiao: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Qiumei Huang: Conceptualization, Investigation. Wenyi Huang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Resources, Methodology. Guoqiang Zhang: Investigation, Conceptualization, Supervision. Dongdong Li: Investigation, Supervision. Zhuwei Zhang: Investigation, Supervision. Hao Cheng: Supervision, Visualization. Jun Feng: Conceptualization, Validation, Funding acquisition, Supervision. Lijun Li: Visualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Open Project of the Guangxi Key Laboratory of Green Processing of Sugar Resources (GXTZY202006) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 82260701, 82460704).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.125998.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
